summary: Gruber dissects the DOJ's renewed push against Apple over iPhone encryption following the Pensacola naval base shooting investigation. He argues that the NYT's framing incorrectly suggests Apple could unlock iPhones but chooses not to, when in reality modern iPhones are designed with no backdoor capability. He criticizes FBI Director Wray and AG Barr for deliberately mischaracterizing Apple's position to build public support for anti-encryption legislation. Gruber emphasizes that what law enforcement claims to want — a backdoor only good guys can use — is mathematically impossible, and calls on news media to stop publishing these claims without expert rebuttal.
key_points: The FBI getting into the Pensacola shooter's iPhone should not be used to argue the system worked — the implicit assumption that impenetrable encryption is unacceptable is the real danger
The NYT framing that Apple 'stopped allowing law enforcement into phones' is wrong — Apple didn't change its cooperation, it changed its engineering to make phones genuinely secure with no backdoor
Barr and Wray's strategy is to publicly shame Apple as aiding terrorists to build political pressure for anti-encryption legislation
A backdoor that only 'good guys' can use is mathematically impossible, and newsrooms have a responsibility to note this when publishing law enforcement claims
Apple cooperated fully with everything technically possible — the DOJ's real demand is that Apple make future iPhones insecure
money_quotes: Strong encryption is, on the whole, a good thing, and should remain legal — regardless whether there are known ways to circumvent it.

The difference between 2014 and today isn't that Apple previously was cooperative with law enforcement requests and now is not — the difference is that modern iPhones can't be 'unlocked' the way older ones could, because the security on modern iPhones is so much better now.
Apple did not defy anyone here. They chose, years ago, to design secure systems that have no backdoors to unlock.
The message from Barr and Wray to Apple is implicitly this: If you won't add backdoors to your devices we're going to keep saying you're aiding terrorists and deviant criminals.
Saying you want technology companies to make a backdoor that only 'good guys' can use is like saying you want guns that only 'good guys' can fire.
You might as well say that you want Apple to come up with a way for 1 + 1 to equal 3.
Apple cooperated in every way they technically could. The DOJ is not asking for Apple's cooperation unlocking existing iPhones — they're asking Apple to make future iPhones insecure.
key_insight: The DOJ's encryption fight is not about Apple refusing to cooperate — it's about the government demanding Apple engineer insecurity into future devices, something that is both mathematically impossible to do safely and deliberately misrepresented by law enforcement to the public.
